summary: Stuck key on the touch panel
meaning: F2E1 means the touch interface reports a key held down. The dishwasher will not respond to new commands until the stuck key state clears.
F3E1 means the wash thermistor sent an invalid reading. The control cannot verify water temperature for heat steps and drying efficiency.
F4E1 indicates the turbidity or optical water sensor failed self-check. Auto soil sensing cycles cannot adjust wash time without this input.

summary: Float switch open—suds or overflow path
meaning: F5E1 means the float switch circuit read open when the control expected continuity. Suds overflow or a stuck float can trigger this safety code.
F6E1 means the water inlet valve did not respond correctly when the control commanded fill. The tub may stay dry or fill very slowly.
F7E1 is a communication-specific fault for the soil-sensing module—distinct from F4E1 optical failure. Auto cycles may default to maximum time.
Code 22 on Maytag French-door and side-by-side refrigerators means the freezer evaporator fan did not report rotation. Cold air stops circulating from the freezer into the fresh food section.
Code 23 indicates the adaptive defrost system detected an open defrost heater circuit. Frost will accumulate on the evaporator until airflow fails.
Code 24 means the defrost thermostat did not close when the heater energized. The board interprets this as a failed defrost temperature path.
Code 25 appears when a defrost cycle exceeded the maximum allowed time without the evaporator thermistor reporting thaw completion.

summary: Compressor relay or start device fault
meaning: Code 26 means the compressor did not start when the control energized the start device. Both sections warm when the sealed system is not pumping.
Code 27 means the freezer compartment thermistor sent an out-of-range signal. Defrost timing and compressor run length depend on accurate freezer temperature feedback.
Code 28 indicates the refrigerator section thermistor failed. The control may over-cool or under-cool the fresh food area without valid readings.

summary: Ice maker optics or position fault
meaning: Code 29 appears on models with optical ice level sensing. The control cannot confirm ice maker position or bin level.
Code 30 means the motorized damper between freezer and refrigerator did not open. The fresh food section warms while the freezer may still cool.
Code 31 means the in-door ice chute flap did not open or close on command. Cubes may jam or warm air enters the freezer through the chute.

summary: Dispenser water valve or switch fault
meaning: Code 32 indicates the dispenser water system failed—valve, switch, or heater circuit on chilled-water models.

summary: Water filter status or flow fault
meaning: Code 33 relates to the water filter circuit—either excessive restriction, missing filter, or RFID mismatch on models with filter sensing.
Code 34 means the control detected poor cooling performance consistent with a sealed system issue—low refrigerant, restriction, or weak compressor pumping.
Code 35 means the dispenser or console UI lost communication with the main control. Temperature displays and ice/water requests may freeze.

summary: Main control to UI serial link fault
meaning: Code 36 is a broader communication fault between the main machine control and distributed boards—UI, ice maker, or damper modules.
F03 on Maytag wall ovens and ranges means the oven temperature sensor (RTD) circuit is open. The board cannot regulate bake or broil without a valid probe signal.
F04 means the oven temperature sensor reads a short circuit. The control disables heat to prevent uncontrolled temperature rise.
F05 appears on self-cleaning Maytag ovens when the door latch cannot lock or unlock on command. Clean and convection cycles may be blocked until the latch reports correct position.
F06 means the door lock switch circuit failed verification. The ERC detected an unexpected open or short in the lock position feedback.
F07 specifically targets the lock motor output circuit. The ERC commanded latch movement but did not detect motor response.
F08 means the oven cooling fan did not start when required. Electronic controls and door components depend on airflow during heat operation.
F09 is a safety over-temperature fault. The ERC detected oven temperature above the safe limit—often from a welded relay or failed sensor reading low.
F10 indicates the oven heated without proportional control—similar to F09 on many Maytag platforms. The ERC shuts down all heat outputs.
F11 means the oven user interface detected a short on the keypad matrix. Buttons may be unresponsive or trigger randomly.
F12 is a stuck-key fault distinct from F11 short. One button appears continuously pressed, blocking other inputs.
F13 means the electronic range control failed a memory checksum at power-up. Stored calibration and timing tables may be corrupt.
F14 is a secondary EEPROM data fault on the same ERC platform. Cooking functions may be unpredictable until the board is replaced.
F15 indicates internal communication failure within the ERC or between ERC and the touch UI. Display may freeze or show garbage characters.

summary: Communication error between ERC and UI
meaning: F16 specifically flags lost serial communication between the display UI and the ERC—common after replacing only one of the two assemblies.

summary: Probe or meat thermometer circuit fault
meaning: F17 appears on models with a temperature probe jack. The ERC detected an open or short in the external meat probe circuit during convection roast or probe bake.

EF0 on Frigidaire front-load washers means the aqua control sensor in the base pan detected moisture, or excessive foam mimicked a leak. The door may lock and the drain pump can run until the condition clears.
EF1 means water could not drain within the expected time—the drain filter is blocked or the pump cannot move water. Frigidaire Electrolux platforms stop the cycle to prevent standing water.
EF2 is an over-sudsing fault detected during drain phases. Foam can trip aqua control and prevent the drum from reaching proper spin speed.
EF3 means the aqua control system latched into safety mode—similar to EF0 but often after repeated foam or leak events. The machine may run the drain pump continuously.

summary: Low incoming water pressure or flow meter fault
meaning: EF4 appears when fill pressure is too low or the flow meter sees no pulse during fill. The washer cannot confirm adequate water entry.

summary: Hot water inlet NTC temperature fault
meaning: EF5 means the hot water inlet thermistor read out of range. Temperature-controlled fills on Electrolux-built Frigidaire washers cannot proceed without valid hot-side feedback.

summary: Motor triac or drive circuit fault
meaning: EF6 indicates the motor drive triac or inverter stage faulted. The drum may not turn during wash or spin even though the door is locked.
EF7 means the door lock circuit failed—often triggered by foam, leaks, or a broken latch motor. The washer will not fill or spin until lock feedback is valid.
EF8 means the pressure switch or transducer did not report a valid water level during fill or drain.
EF9 means the tachometer on the motor did not send speed feedback for several seconds. Spin and wash profiles stop without RPM confirmation.
EF10 is a secondary pressure fault—the switch state did not change when water entered or left the tub.

summary: Heating circuit or NTC wash temperature fault
meaning: EF11 means the washer could not heat water to the selected temperature—common on models with internal heaters or strict temperature monitoring.

summary: Communication error between UI and control
meaning: EF12 means the display board and main control lost serial communication. Buttons and cycle progress may freeze.

summary: Control EEPROM or memory verification failed
meaning: EF13 indicates corrupted non-volatile memory on the main control. Cycle tables and calibration may be invalid.
EF14 means repeated unbalance detection—the drum could not reach spin speed after multiple redistribution attempts.
E01 means the door switch never reported closed. Frigidaire dryers block the motor until the plunger is fully depressed.
E02 indicates a continuously activated keypad. Cycle selection is blocked.
E03 means the exhaust temperature sensor circuit is open. Heat modulation stops without valid exhaust feedback.
E04 means auto-dry moisture bars read open. Sensor dry cycles may end too early or not start.
E05 indicates the exhaust thermal fuse opened from overheating—usually restricted vent airflow.
E06 means the user interface and main control lost communication.
E07 is a shorted moisture sensor circuit—opposite of E04.
E08 is a static-sensitive keypad fault in low-humidity conditions.
E09 appears when UI and main control firmware revisions do not match after partial replacement.
E10 means line voltage fell outside the safe operating window.
E11 means the UI detected a hard short on the keypad matrix.
E12 indicates a drive relay on the main control failed self-test.
E13 means control memory failed checksum at power-up.
E14 is a second checksum fault path on the control platform.
E15 means stored cycle data read back invalid from EEPROM.
i30 means water collected in the bottom pan and lifted the styrofoam float. The drain pump may run continuously as an anti-flood measure.
i40 indicates water could not drain within the timeout—same family as i20 with a secondary drain fault flag.

summary: Drain pump or wash motor failure
meaning: i50 means the drain or circulation motor failed to start or report feedback.
i60 means the dishwasher could not heat water to the required temperature for wash or dry.
i70 indicates the wash thermistor sent invalid readings.

summary: Vent fan or drying system fault
meaning: i80 relates to the vented drying system—fan not running or vent stuck open/closed.

summary: Water inlet valve or fill timeout
meaning: i90 means inadequate water entered the tub within the fill window.
iA0 indicates sudden main control board failure—iC0 on some displays is the same class of fault.
iB0 means the door switch circuit failed—related to CL door switch codes on some models.
iC0 indicates configuration data on the control is invalid or missing after power loss.
iD0 means the detergent dispenser did not open on command—SD code family on some units.
iE0 indicates the turbidity sensor failed—TU code on related models.
iF0 is grouped with i20 and i40 as a drain restriction code on Frigidaire support charts.

summary: Relay or output driver fault on control
meaning: iG0 means a control board output relay failed verification during self-test.
iH0 appears when control firmware versions conflict—often after replacing only one board.
H1 on Frigidaire Gallery and Professional refrigerators means the freezer thermistor signal failed.
H2 indicates the refrigerator compartment thermistor failed.
H3 means the freezer evaporator fan did not report rotation.
H4 means the motorized damper between freezer and refrigerator did not move.
H5 indicates an open defrost heater or defrost circuit failure.
H6 means defrost did not complete correctly—thermostat or timeout related.

summary: Ice maker motor or position fault
meaning: H7 relates to ice maker harvest or optics feedback failure.

summary: Water valve or dispenser flow fault
meaning: H8 indicates water valve, filter restriction, or dispenser circuit fault.

summary: Compressor start device or relay fault
meaning: H9 means the compressor did not start when commanded.
HA means a door was detected open too long or the switch circuit failed.
HB is a high-temperature alarm in the freezer—performance issue rather than sensor-only.

summary: High temperature in fresh food section
meaning: HC means fresh food compartment exceeded safe temperature threshold.
HD indicates the dispenser or console UI lost communication with the main board.

summary: Main control to sub-board link fault
meaning: HE is a serial communication fault between main control and distributed modules.
HF is a combined system fault flag—often defrost performance or firmware integrity on SY/EF platforms.
F03 means the oven temperature sensor circuit is open on Frigidaire ranges and wall ovens.
F04 indicates a shorted oven sensor—the ERC disables heat.
F05 means the door latch could not lock or unlock for clean or bake.
F06 means lock position feedback failed verification.
F07 targets the lock motor output specifically.
F08 means the oven cooling fan did not start when required.
F09 is a safety shutdown from excessive cavity temperature.
F10 indicates uncontrolled heating—ERC shuts all outputs.
F11 means a short on the keypad matrix.
F12 is a stuck-key fault without hard short.
F13 means EEPROM checksum failed at power-up.
F14 is secondary EEPROM data corruption on the ERC.
F15 indicates internal ERC or UI communication failure.

summary: UI to ERC serial link fault
meaning: F16 means lost serial link between touch UI and ERC.
F17 appears on probe-equipped models when the meat probe circuit is open or shorted.

summary: Stuck button on the user interface
meaning: F2 on Kenmore Elite HE front-load washers (110.41382, 110.41952 series) means the Central Control Unit sees a user-interface button held down. The washer will not accept cycle changes until the stuck key clears.
F3 means the Suds Control Circuit or pressure transducer on the Central Control Unit reads a short. Water level detection fails and the washer may stop fill or drain steps immediately.
F4 appears when the pressure switch does not release during the drain portion of a cycle. The CCU still sees tub water when the drain pump should have emptied the drum.

summary: Water temperature sensor out of range
meaning: F5 means the water temperature sensor (NTC) on Kenmore HE washers reported a value outside the expected range during the water heating step. Heated cycles may abort before reaching target temperature.
F6 means the door lock assembly could not achieve a locked position after multiple attempts. Kenmore HE front-loaders block fill and spin until the latch confirms the door is secure.
F7 means the Motor Control Unit detected an internal processor fault. The drive system stops and the drum will not agitate or spin until the MCU resets or is replaced.

summary: Water inlet valve stuck open or closed
meaning: F8 means the CCU detected a water inlet valve that would not close or would not open on command. Fill may stop mid-cycle or continue when the washer is powered off.
F9 reports an overflow condition on Kenmore HE front-load washers. The pressure switch sees tub water above the safe fill level and the drain pump may run continuously while the cycle halts.
F10 means the Motor Control Unit failed its EEPROM memory check. Drive commands from the CCU are ignored until the MCU resets or is replaced.

summary: Communication fault between CCU and MCU
meaning: F11 is the most common Kenmore HE4t and HE5t fault—it means serial data between the Central Control Unit and Motor Control Unit was lost. Rinse and spin may stop mid-cycle when the boards cannot exchange motor commands.

summary: Dispenser motor cannot reach latch position
meaning: F12 means the detergent dispenser motor could not drive the diverter cam to the commanded position. Auto-dispense cycles on Kenmore HE models may skip bleach or fabric-softener steps.

summary: Dispenser motor current out of range
meaning: F13 means the CCU measured abnormal current from the dispenser motor—either too high from a mechanical stall or too low from an open winding.
F14 means the Central Control Unit detected a corrupted EEPROM or memory verification failure. Cycle parameters cannot be stored reliably until the board is reset or replaced.
F15 is a secondary CCU memory fault on some Kenmore HE platforms—the control cannot pass its startup integrity check and may flash F15 before any cycle will start.

summary: Door lock cannot lock or unlock
meaning: F16 means the door lock mechanism failed to complete a lock or unlock cycle. The CCU commanded the latch but did not receive the expected position feedback within the timeout window.
F01 on Kenmore 110-series dryers means the main electronic control failed its internal memory check. Heat, motor, and moisture-sensing outputs stop until the board resets or is replaced.

summary: Key stuck on the user interface
meaning: F02 means the console detects a keypad or touch button held continuously. The Kenmore dryer will not start a cycle while it thinks a selection key is pressed.
F03 means the exhaust thermistor sent an open-circuit reading. The control cannot modulate heat safely without exhaust temperature feedback and may shut off the burner or element.
F04 indicates the moisture sensor circuit is open. Auto-dry cycles on Kenmore dryers rely on the two metal strips inside the front bulkhead to detect dampness and end the cycle.
F05 means the door switch never reported closed. Kenmore dryers block motor and heat circuits until the door strike depresses the switch plunger fully.
F06 means the user interface board and main control lost serial communication. Display, knob input, and heat commands cannot synchronize on Kenmore electronic-console dryers.
F07 is the opposite of F04—the moisture sensor circuit reads a short. Auto-dry may end immediately or not advance because the board sees constant wetness.
F08 is a static-sensitive keypad fault on Kenmore dryers. Dry winter air or synthetic clothing can discharge into the touch panel and momentarily confuse the UI.
F09 appears when the user interface and main control report mismatched software versions. After a partial board replacement the two assemblies may not communicate correctly.
F10 means the control detected line voltage outside the safe 108–132 VAC window. Motor and heat outputs are disabled to protect components.

summary: Motor relay stuck on the control board
meaning: F11 means the main control detected a motor relay that would not release. The drum may continue turning when the cycle should be paused—a safety concern on Kenmore electronic dryers.
F12 on Kenmore gas dryers means the gas valve circuit did not respond correctly. The burner will not ignite and clothes remain wet after a full cycle.
F13 on select Kenmore dryers means the lint screen presence sensor circuit failed. Some models use this input to verify the lint filter is installed before heat is enabled.
F14 means the main control detected a heating element relay that would not open. The dryer may overheat or continue heating when the cycle should be in cool-down.
F15 means the exhaust thermistor reads a short circuit. The control disables heat because it cannot trust exhaust temperature feedback.
F2 on Whirlpool-built Kenmore dishwashers (665.xxxxx) means water reached the base pan and tripped the anti-flood float switch. The control stops filling and may run the drain pump continuously.
F3 means the tub water thermistor is reading abnormally—open or shorted. Kenmore 665 dishwashers use this NTC near the heating element to regulate wash and dry temperatures.
F4 means the main wash motor did not start or did not reach expected speed. Spray arms will not rotate and dishes stay dirty after the wash phase times out.
F5 means the turbidity or soil sensor in the sump reported an invalid reading. Auto-wash cycles on newer Kenmore 665 models use this sensor to adjust cycle length based on water clarity.
F6 means the dishwasher did not fill with water within the allowed time. The inlet valve never opened or household pressure was too low for the fill step.
F7 means the flow meter turbine did not pulse when the inlet valve opened. The control uses this sensor to verify water is actually entering the tub—not just that the valve coil energized.
F8 means the drain pump ran but water remained in the tub after the drain timeout. Standing water at the end of a cycle or cancel/drain triggers this code on Kenmore 665 dishwashers.
F9 means the diverter motor that routes water between upper and lower spray arms did not reach the commanded position. One rack may get no spray during the affected portion of the cycle.

F10 means the drying vent fan did not start when commanded. Kenmore dishwashers with a vent fan use it to pull moist air out during the dry phase—F10 leaves dishes wet and may trip related heat faults.

summary: Touchpad cannot talk to main control
meaning: F11 means the touchpad user interface lost communication with the main control board inside the door. The display may freeze or alternate between F11 and blank segments.
F12 means the detergent dispenser did not open during the wash step. Dishes may come out greasy when the main wash detergent never entered the tub.
F13 means the main electronic control failed its EEPROM memory verification. Cycle programs cannot load reliably and the dishwasher may not start any cycle.
F14 means the touchpad membrane or button panel failed self-test. Button presses may not register or the panel may report multiple keys at once.
F15 means the heating element circuit did not draw expected current during heat step. Wash water stays cold and the dry phase fails on Kenmore 665 dishwashers.
F16 means the door switch circuit reads open when the control expects the door to be latched. The dishwasher will not fill or run wash steps until the switch confirms closure.

summary: Pantry compartment sensor 2 out of range
meaning: Code 22 on Kenmore 106-series refrigerators means pantry compartment sensor 2 resistance is outside the normal range—above 45K ohms when cold. The ice maker module cannot read accurate temperature for the refrigerated drawer zone.

summary: Ice maker switch not cycling during harvest
meaning: Code 23 means the board did not see the ice maker switch cycle during the harvest rotation. The harvest cam should momentarily open the switch at home position—without that pulse the module thinks the gearbox is out of sync.

summary: Ice tray sensor resistance too high
meaning: Code 24 means the ice tray thermistor resistance is above the normal upper limit—greater than about 45K ohms. The module cannot determine mold temperature for freeze and harvest timing.

summary: Ice tray sensor resistance too low
meaning: Code 25 is the low-resistance counterpart to code 24—the ice tray thermistor reads below about 660 ohms, indicating a shorted or damaged sensor circuit.
Code 26 means the ice maker drive motor ran longer than allowed without completing its rotation. The gearbox did not return to the start position within the module timeout window.

summary: Ice maker motor current too high
meaning: Code 27 means the ice maker motor is drawing more current than expected—usually from a mechanical bind or failing motor bearings inside the gearbox.

summary: Water inlet solenoid current too high
meaning: Code 28 means the ice maker water inlet solenoid is drawing excessive current when energized. The module protects the driver circuit by logging code 28 and skipping fill.

summary: Water inlet solenoid current too low
meaning: Code 29 means the fill solenoid drew less current than expected—often an open coil, poor connection, or no water reaching the valve when fill was commanded.

summary: Ice mold heater not reaching temperature
meaning: Code 30 means the ice mold heater failed to heat the mold to release temperature during harvest. The module timed out waiting for the thermistor to confirm the mold warmed enough to eject cubes.
Code 31 means the harvest motor current or speed fell outside the calibrated range during the eject stroke. The module detected abnormal motor performance compared to factory limits.

summary: Harvest thermistor out of calibrated range
meaning: Code 32 means the harvest thermistor reading during the eject phase was outside the expected resistance band. The module uses this sensor to confirm the mold released ice before returning to freeze.

summary: Fill tube heater out of range
meaning: Code 33 means the fill tube heater resistance or current is outside the acceptable range. A cold fill tube freezes incoming water and prevents the mold from filling—code 33 catches heater circuit failures early.

Code 34 means the mold heater current during harvest was higher or lower than the module expects. Unlike code 30's timeout, code 34 flags marginal heater performance still within time but outside electrical limits.

summary: Harvest motor speed out of range
meaning: Code 35 means the harvest motor speed profile did not match calibrated limits—too fast or too slow through the eject stroke. The module uses timing feedback to detect slipping gears or weak motors.

Code 36 means the harvest thermistor failed a range check distinct from code 32—typically comparing mold temperature delta before and after the heater cycle. The module concludes the sensor cannot reliably report release temperature.
F03 on Kenmore 790-series ranges means the oven temperature sensor (RTD) circuit is open. The electronic range control cannot regulate bake or broil without a valid probe signal.
F04 means the oven temperature sensor reads a short circuit. The ERC disables heat to prevent uncontrolled temperature rise.
F05 appears on self-cleaning Kenmore ovens when the door latch cannot lock or unlock on command. Clean and convection cycles may be blocked until the latch reports correct position.
F06 means the door lock switch circuit failed verification. The ERC detected an unexpected open or short in the lock position feedback.
F07 specifically targets the lock motor output circuit. The ERC commanded latch movement but did not detect motor response.
F08 means the oven cooling fan did not start when required. Electronic controls and door components depend on airflow during heat operation on Kenmore wall ovens.
F09 is a safety over-temperature fault. The ERC detected oven temperature above the safe limit—often from a welded relay or failed sensor reading low.
F10 indicates the oven heated without proportional control—similar to F09 on Kenmore 790 platforms. The ERC shuts down all heat outputs.
F11 means the oven user interface detected a short on the keypad matrix. Buttons may be unresponsive or trigger randomly.
F12 is a stuck-key fault distinct from F11 short. One button appears continuously pressed, blocking other inputs on the Kenmore range console.
F13 means the electronic range control failed a memory checksum at power-up. Stored calibration and timing tables may be corrupt.
F14 is a secondary EEPROM data fault on the Kenmore ERC platform. Cooking functions may be unpredictable until the board is replaced.
F15 indicates internal communication failure within the ERC or between ERC and the touch UI. Display may freeze or show garbage characters.

summary: Communication error between ERC and UI
meaning: F16 specifically flags lost serial communication between the display UI and the ERC—common after replacing only one of the two assemblies on Kenmore ranges.

summary: Probe or meat thermometer circuit fault
meaning: F17 appears on Kenmore models with a temperature probe jack. The ERC detected an open or short in the external meat probe circuit during convection roast or probe bake.

summary: No active faults in diagnostic memory
meaning: E000 corresponds to GE's E00 state—diagnostics found no active fault codes in memory. If the washer misbehaves, an intermittent issue may have cleared or the failure is outside the self-test list.

summary: Main control communication or memory fault
meaning: E001 indicates the main control or motor control unit failed communication or memory check—similar to GE CCU faults on 24-inch front-load platforms.
E002 maps to GE's door lock fault—the machine will not fill or spin until the door lock confirms closed.
E003 means excessive current on the drive motor—often overload, jam, or failing motor inverter.
E004 indicates the inlet valve circuit failed—valves not opening, stuck open, or not reporting correctly.

summary: Drain pump problem or long drain
meaning: E005 means drain timed out—the pump ran but water remained, matching GE 5E on front-load washers.
E006 corresponds to motor speed faults—drum commanded to spin but RPM feedback never reached target.

summary: Pressure sensor or water level fault
meaning: E007 means the pressure switch or sensor did not report a valid water level during fill or drain.

summary: Heater relay error on main board
meaning: E008 indicates the heater relay on the main control failed—internal heat models cannot warm water.
E009 means line voltage dropped below the safe minimum—often shared circuits or loose connections.
E010 indicates supply voltage exceeded the upper safe limit.
E011 maps to GE tE—temperature sensor out of range on heat or monitoring circuits.

summary: Leakage detected in tub or base
meaning: E012 is GE's leakage fault—moisture detected where it should not be, often at the door boot or sump.
E013 means the water level exceeded maximum—pressure switch saw overfill or inlet valve stuck open.
E014 is GE's unbalanced load fault—spin aborted when weight distribution prevented safe RPM.

summary: Main control EEPROM or memory fault
meaning: E001 indicates the dryer main control failed memory verification at startup.
E002 means the door switch never reported closed.
E003 indicates open exhaust temperature sensor circuit.
E004 means auto-dry moisture bars read open.
E005 indicates thermal protection opened—usually from restricted venting.

summary: Communication error between UI and control
meaning: E006 means user interface lost communication with main control.
E007 is shorted moisture sensor circuit.

summary: Gas valve or igniter circuit fault
meaning: E008 on gas GE dryers indicates heat circuit failure—igniter, valve coils, or flame sensor.
E009 means stuck or shorted user interface key.
E010 indicates severe exhaust restriction detected—similar to vent safety codes on modern GE dryers.
E011 is static on the touch UI in low humidity.

summary: Drive motor or belt switch fault
meaning: E012 means motor not running or belt switch open on belt-drive GE dryers.
E013 indicates control EEPROM checksum failure.
E014 is second checksum fault path on control.

summary: Software mismatch between UI and control
meaning: E015 means UI and main control firmware versions conflict.
C1 means pump-out during drain exceeded two minutes per GE factory definition.
C2 indicates total pump-out exceeded seven minutes—more severe than C1.
C3 means the dishwasher will not drain after reset sequence per GE support.

summary: Float switch stuck or double fill after outage
meaning: C4 appears when machine filled twice after power failure or float switch stuck—GE official definition.
C5 means pump-out completed faster than expected—often pressure or float sensor anomaly.
C6 means water temperature below GE minimum—dishwashers need at least 120°F for proper wash.
C7 means the water temperature sensor circuit failed per GE fault table.
C8 indicates the detergent cup could not open fully—GE official definition.
C9 maps to distributed board communication failures on GE dishwasher platforms.

summary: Wash pump or motor circuit fault
meaning: C10 indicates wash motor failed to start or report feedback.
C11 means dishwasher could not heat water to required temperature.
C12 indicates inadequate water entered within fill window—related to H2O low water codes.
C13 means door switch circuit failed—washer won't start fill until latched.

summary: Turbidity or pressure sensor invalid signal
meaning: C14 maps to pressure or turbidity sensor sending invalid data—similar to GE PrS code family.

summary: Main control EEPROM or memory fault
meaning: C15 indicates main control memory or processor fault on extended GE diagnostic list.
FF on GE French-door refrigerators means the freezer evaporator fan failed to run or report speed.
H1 indicates freezer temperature sensor signal failure.
H2 means refrigerator compartment thermistor failed.
H3 indicates defrost heater circuit failure.
H4 means motorized damper did not open between compartments.
H5 relates to defrost not completing—thermostat or duration.
H6 is high-temperature alert in fresh food section.
H7 means freezer exceeded safe temperature.

summary: Ice maker harvest or motor fault
meaning: H8 indicates ice maker motor or harvest feedback failure.
H9 means compressor did not start when commanded.
HA means door detected open beyond timeout.

summary: Water filter restriction or RFID fault
meaning: HB relates to water filter status or excessive restriction on GE dispenser models.

summary: Dispenser water valve or line fault
meaning: HC indicates water dispenser circuit fault.
HD means dispenser or console UI lost communication.
HE is serial communication failure between main control and modules.
F03 means oven temperature sensor circuit is open.
F04 indicates shorted oven sensor.
F05 means door latch cannot lock or unlock.
F06 means lock position feedback failed.
F07 targets lock motor output failure.
F08 means cooling fan did not start when required.
F09 is safety shutdown from excessive cavity temperature.
F10 indicates uncontrolled heating.
F11 means short on keypad matrix.
F12 is stuck-key without hard short.
F13 means EEPROM checksum failed at power-up.
F14 is secondary EEPROM corruption.
F15 indicates internal ERC or UI communication failure.

summary: UI to ERC serial link fault
meaning: F16 means lost serial link between touch UI and ERC.
F17 appears when meat probe circuit is open or shorted on probe-equipped GE ovens.

summary: Inlet thermistor reading out of range
meaning: tE1 is a thermistor sub-fault on LG condenser and heat-pump dryers—the sensor at the inlet heat exchanger sent resistance outside the board's valid range. Unlike exhaust tE, tE1 points to the cooler inlet path where lint and condensate collect.

summary: Door lock cannot release after cycle
meaning: u5 reports Unable to Unlock—the door lock motor energized to release but the latch never reported an open position within the timeout. Water may still be in the tub, which is why LG blocks the door until the fault clears or you drain manually.

summary: Child Lock active—not a mechanical fault
meaning: CL is Child Lock, not a failure code. LG disables cycle selection and temperature buttons so children cannot start or change a wash. The display may flash CL when any locked button is pressed.

summary: Inlet water flow too low during fill
meaning: 6E is LG's inlet flow error—the fill valve opened but the flow meter or level switch did not see enough water within the programmed window. The cycle aborts before wash to avoid running the pump dry.

CHE is a Compressor Error on LG linear inverter refrigerators—the main board lost valid feedback from the inverter or compressor windings. Cooling may stop while the display shows CHE, and freezer temps climb within hours.

summary: Control Lock enabled on the display
meaning: CLE is Control Lock Engaged—the same function as CL on other LG displays. Water and ice dispensers and temperature keys are disabled until lock is cleared. It is not a cooling or sensor fault.

summary: Flood sensor triggered in base pan
meaning: E22 is Bosch's flood protection code—the polystyrene float in the base pan detected water. The inlet valve shuts immediately and the display shows E22 to keep water off your kitchen floor.

summary: Vent motor or drying fan circuit fault
meaning: i20 on Frigidaire dishwashers indicates the vent motor or drying fan circuit failed—the control energized the vent assembly during the dry phase but did not detect proper operation. Dishes may finish wet when i20 appears.

F21 on Kenmore front-load washers is the long-drain fault inherited from Whirlpool Duet platform—the drain pump ran longer than about eight minutes without emptying the tub. The washer pauses and locks the door until water is removed.

E2 on GE washers indicates a fill timeout—the control opened inlet valves but the water level switch never confirmed expected fill within the programmed window. GE halts before agitation to avoid dry drum operation.

summary: Water inlet flood or flow fault
meaning: F8E4 on Maytag dishwashers reports a water inlet flood condition—the overfill float or flow sensor detected excess water entering or not draining during fill. Maytag stops the cycle to limit tub overflow.

summary: Door or lid lock circuit fault
meaning: F5 on Maytag washers indicates a door or lid lock fault—the control did not confirm a locked position before fill or spin. On front-loaders it maps to door latch circuits; on top-load Bravos models it relates to the lid lock plunger.

summary: Water not draining from the drum
meaning: ND is Samsung's No Drain error—the drain pump ran but the water level sensor still read high after the allotted drain window. The washer stops before high-speed spin to avoid running with a full tub.

summary: Drain fault—same family as ND and 5C; pump timeout with water in tub
meaning: Samsung 5E is a drain error in the same family as ND and 5C on front-load washers—the control ran the drain pump for the programmed interval (often 15 minutes or more on some cycles) but the water level sensor still detected water in the tub. Display may show 5E, SE, or ND depending on firmware region. On WF45T6000AW and similar, service access is through the lower front access panel with a black emergency drain hose and twist-off filter cap—not the top-loader path. 5E is not a fill fault (see 4C) or balance fault (see UB).

summary: AC power frequency out of tolerance
meaning: 9C8 reports the control detected mains frequency outside Samsung's acceptable band—often below 45 Hz or above 65 Hz on the sensing circuit. Washers on extension cords, generator backup, or damaged supply legs see 9C8 at startup.
8C flags the MEMS imbalance sensor—the accelerometer on the tub frame that monitors vibration during spin. Samsung cannot safely ramp RPM without valid MEMS data, so spin aborts and 8C displays.

3C indicates the motor position sensor did not report expected rotor position at startup or during load sensing. Samsung's inverter needs hall sensor feedback to commutate the brushless motor—3C stops the cycle before agitation.

summary: Excess suds slowing drain and spin
meaning: SUD (also shown as 5Ud) means Samsung detected excessive suds during drain or spin. The washer extends drain time trying to break foam and may pause with SUD on the display until suds level drops.
SC reports lost communication between the main PCB and subsystems—often the UI board, inverter, or Wi-Fi module. Buttons may freeze and SC flashes when the handshake fails at power-on or mid-cycle.
UR is Samsung's Unbalanced Reload code—the drum could not redistribute laundry enough to safely reach spin speed. The washer pauses and displays UR so you can open the door and rearrange items.

nF (also shown as 4E on newer displays) means Not Filling—the inlet valves opened but the water level sensor never saw a rise within the fill window. Samsung aborts before agitation to protect the drum from dry running.

SE is a general System Error on Samsung washers—often paired with motor faults 5E or 3C on the same cycle. The main board logged an unspecified fault in the drive or communication subsystem and halted the cycle.

summary: Water level too high in the tub
meaning: OC is Over Level water—the level sensor read high when the control expected normal wash depth, or water kept entering after shutoff. Samsung may run the drain pump continuously trying to lower the level.

summary: Zone wash or half-load position fault
meaning: PC flags a Position Error on Samsung dishwashers with movable wash zones—Zone Booster or WaterWall reflector systems. The control commanded travel along the bottom rail but the position sensor never confirmed the expected location.
5C is Samsung's drain fault—the pump ran but water level remained high after the drain window. Same family as 5E display variant; water stays in the tub bottom and the cycle stops before dry.
3C on Samsung dishwashers indicates the wash motor position sensor did not report expected rotor alignment—the circulation motor cannot start wash spray until the hall sensor confirms position.

summary: Door circuit open at cycle start
meaning: DC on Samsung dryers means the door switch circuit read open when the control expected closed. Heat and drum motion are blocked until the latch confirms the door is sealed—distinct from washer DC imbalance code.

summary: Exhaust vent restriction at ninety percent
meaning: CL9 (C9 variant) reports roughly ninety percent exhaust airflow restriction on Samsung vent-sense models. The dryer measures blower strain; at this level heat cycles shorten and CL9 warns before a full block triggers shutdown.

summary: Gas supply not detected on gas models
meaning: H01 appears on Samsung gas dryers when the control does not detect gas supply pressure or the gas valve failed to open during a heat request. Electric models never show H01—confirm your unit is gas before troubleshooting fuel.

summary: Vent airflow blocked about ninety percent
meaning: D90 is Samsung's vent blockage warning at roughly ninety percent restriction. Heat may cycle off early and drying times stretch far beyond normal until airflow is restored.
3C on Samsung dryers indicates the drive motor hall sensor did not report valid rotor position at startup. The drum will not turn until the inverter receives position feedback from the motor.

Sd (also shown as 5d on some displays) is Suds Detected—a suds lock fault. The washer sensed too much foam during drain and extended the pump time trying to break suds. Whirlpool pauses the cycle until suds drop or you intervene.

summary: Door failed to unlock after cycle
meaning: F5E3 means the door lock motor tried to release but the latch never reported unlocked. Whirlpool front-loaders block the door when water remains or when the lock motor fails on the release stroke.

summary: Drain exceeded time—water remained in tub after pump timeout
meaning: Whirlpool F9 E1 means drain time exceeded—the pump ran but the tub did not empty within the programmed window on VMW vertical modular washer platforms. This code is most common on top-load HE units such as WTW5000DW and WTW8127LW (Cabrio/VMW family). F9 E1 is the top-load analog to front-load F21: both are drain timeouts, but access paths differ—top loads often use an impeller wash plate and rear pump access rather than the front coin trap on WFW5605MW. SD (suds) and F8 E1 (long fill) frequently appear in the same service history when drain and fill paths share pressure switch issues.

summary: Long fill—water did not reach target level within 13-minute timeout
meaning: Whirlpool F8 E1 displays as LO FL (low flow) on many VMW vertical modular washer platforms. The control opens inlet valves and waits up to roughly 13 minutes for the pressure switch to confirm target fill level; if water enters too slowly or not at all, F8 E1 aborts the cycle. This applies to top-load HE units like WTW8127LW and front-load WFW5605MW sharing VMW fill logic. Both hot and cold supplies must be available even on cold-only cycles because the washer may pulse hot for temperature mixing. Anti-flood hoses, closed valves, and standpipes below 39 inches are frequent field causes—not the pressure switch as a first replacement.

F5E2 means the door lock motor could not achieve a locked position after multiple attempts. Whirlpool will not fill or spin until the latch confirms closure—you may hear repeated lock buzzing before F5E2 displays.

summary: Pressure switch out of range at idle
meaning: F3E1 indicates the water level pressure switch reported an out-of-range reading when the tub should be empty—or the signal never matched expected fill steps. The control cannot safely open inlet valves without valid level feedback.

summary: Heater did not reach target wash temperature
meaning: F7E1 means the washer could not raise water temperature as programmed—the tub did not pass the minimum heat threshold within the allowed window on a cycle that calls for hot wash water.

summary: Water supply fault during fill attempt
meaning: F8E6 is a water supply error—the control opened inlet valves but flow did not meet minimum rate or stopped unexpectedly mid-fill. Whirlpool aborts before agitation to protect the drum and heater from dry operation.

summary: Fill cycle took too long to complete
meaning: LF is Long Fill—the washer did not reach the expected water level within the fill timeout, usually about six to eight minutes. Both hot and cold must be connected on Duet front-loaders; single-hose installs trigger LF.

E1 on Whirlpool washers signals a Main Control fault—the ACU logged an internal error or lost synchronization with a subsystem. The washer may stop mid-cycle and flash E1 or F1E1 depending on display firmware.

summary: Door switch circuit open at cycle start
meaning: F5 is a door switch fault—the control did not see the door circuit close within the startup window. On some Cabrio and VMT top-loaders F5 relates to lid lock; on front-load Duet models it maps to door latch circuits similar to F22/F5E2.

summary: UI lost communication with main control
meaning: F6E8 reports lost communication between the User Interface board and the Appliance Control Unit. Buttons may be dead, lights flash randomly, or the display shows F6E8 while the washer will not start.

F8E4 means the control opened the detergent dispenser but did not detect the expected water flow or actuator movement through the dispenser chamber. Wash performance drops because main wash detergent never entered the tub.

summary: Vent and fan circuit electrical fault
meaning: F6E1 is a vent/electric fault on Whirlpool dishwashers with a drying fan in the door vent assembly—the control detected an open or shorted circuit when energizing the vent fan during the dry phase.

E1F9 reports the wash temperature sensor (thermistor in the tub or OWI assembly) sent resistance outside the expected range. The control cannot regulate heat or verify sanitizing temperature without valid readings.

E1F1 flags the Optical Water Indicator—the sensor in the tub floor that detects water presence and turbidity. Whirlpool uses OWI feedback for fill verification and soil sensing; E1F1 means the signal was missing or out of range.

FAE4 means the detergent dispenser actuator did not reach the expected position after the control sent open command—related to F8E4 but specifically on models that monitor actuator travel with a position switch.

F4E4 reports the lint screen thermistor—mounted in the lint filter housing—sent an out-of-range temperature signal. Whirlpool uses this sensor on some ventless and console models to detect restricted airflow at the filter slot.

summary: Electronic control memory or relay fault
meaning: F1 on Whirlpool ovens indicates an Electronic Range Control fault—bad EEPROM checksum, stuck relay, or internal board failure. The display may beep and show F1 at idle or lock out bake and broil until power is removed.

F2 means the oven temperature sensor reported cavity temperature above the safe limit—often above 620°F on electric models—or the control detected a runaway heat condition. Whirlpool locks out heat to prevent fire risk.
F1E0 is the modern F/E display for EEPROM failure on the oven Electronic Range Control—the non-volatile memory that stores calibration and cycle data failed checksum at power-on.

summary: Cooling fan not operating when required
meaning: F9E0 reports the oven cooling fan did not run when the control commanded it—typically after broil or self-clean when the control panel and electronics need forced air cooling. Whirlpool locks functions until the fan circuit is verified.

summary: Door lock circuit fault during clean or bake
meaning: F6E1 on Whirlpool ovens indicates a door switch or latch circuit fault—the control did not see the expected door lock position during self-clean, broil vent lock, or standard bake interlock checks.

summary: Display shows 5d instead of Sd—suds lockout
meaning: 5d appears on some Whirlpool LED panels where the letter S looks like the numeral 5—it is the same Suds Detected lockout as Sd, not a separate hardware fault. The machine extended drain trying to break foam before spin.

summary: Circulation pump short circuit—~43 ohms expected at pump pins
meaning: Bosch dishwasher E20 indicates a circulation pump electrical fault—specifically a short circuit or out-of-spec resistance at the pump motor/heater module pins. Service literature cites approximately 43 ohms as the expected reading at the circulation pump connector on heat-pump and standard Bosch sump modules such as SHPM88Z75N and SHEM78B75N. This is NOT Samsung washer E20 or other brands' E20 codes. An impeller jam from glass or seed can mimic E20 by overloading the motor until thermal short detection trips—always clear the filter and rotate the impeller by hand before condemning the pump.
